title: CODE-REVIEW-1: slice-A (nix-openclaw-vm-1fq)
description:
## Review of Slice A by Reviewer 1 (Requirements Alignment)

### Requirements Alignment
- devMode options (enable, openbaoToken, openbaoPort) ✅ defined with correct types
- credproxy-openbao-dev service ✅ starts OpenBao in dev mode with hardening
- credproxy-openbao-provision oneshot ✅ runs full OIDC + KV v2 setup
- Auto-config wiring (effectiveOidcIssuerURL, effectiveVaultAddress) ✅
- credproxy After/Requires on provision ✅

### Critical Gap: Dynamic client_id vs static oidcAudience
The provisioning script generates a dynamic UUID client_id via OpenBao:
  CLIENT_ID=$(bao write -format=json identity/oidc/client/credproxy ... | jq -r '.data.client_id')

OpenBao issues JWTs with aud=<dynamic UUID>. But credproxy config is built at Nix evaluation time with:
  oidcAudience = 'credproxy'  (default, static string)

These will NEVER match. JWT verification will fail for every request. The plan (6ce) acknowledged this: 'audience: <client_id from provisioning>' — but provided no mechanism to propagate the dynamic ID back into the static config file.

Workarounds: (a) provision the client with a pre-set static client_id that matches the NixOS option default, or (b) the provisioning script rewrites the credproxy config with the actual client_id after provisioning.

### User Vision
Plan is to use real OpenBao for OIDC. ✅ This avoids mock code.
But the end-user demo will fail at JWT verification due to the audience gap above. ❌

### MVP Scope
Appropriate complexity. Single provisioning script is correct scope.

### Codebase Quality
- No TODO placeholders ✅
- Hardening on dev server matches credproxy-temporal pattern ✅
- provision service missing StateDirectory (relies on dev service having set it) — acceptable
- ProtectSystem not set on provision service — low-risk since it's a oneshot with User=credproxy

### Validation Checklist
- nix flake check passes ✅ (confirmed by close reason)
- Runtime behavior (JWT verification) will fail due to audience mismatch ❌

## VOTE: REVISE
Reason: Dynamic OpenBao client_id (UUID) cannot match static oidcAudience='credproxy'. JWT verification will fail for every request. The provisioning script must either (a) use a pre-set static client_id matching the NixOS option, or (b) rewrite the credproxy config with the actual client_id. This is a correctness blocker for the end-to-end demo.
close_reason: Review findings addressed in commit ef84e6d on epic/t5b. All critical and minor items resolved. nix flake check passes.

comments.author: David Huu Pham
comments.text: RESOLVED: audience mismatch fixed in ef84e6d. Provisioning script now passes client_id="${cfg.oidcAudience}" (static string, default 'credproxy') to bao write, so JWT aud claim matches credproxy config at evaluation time. VOTE: APPROVE

title: Fix credproxy-lockdown nftables to allow Tailscale/Headscale SSH traffic
description:
The credproxy-lockdown nftables chain (default.nix:569-584) drops ALL new outbound VM connections when credential proxy is enabled. This also blocks Tailscale/Headscale SSH access which the user needs for remote management.

The existing openclaw-vm-firewall table already marks VM traffic with Tailscale forwarding bit (0x00044F43). The lockdown chain needs to exempt Tailscale interface traffic.

Fix: Add a rule before the final drop:
```
# Allow Tailscale/Headscale traffic (SSH and other tailnet services)
iifname "tailscale0" accept
oifname "tailscale0" accept
```

Or better, accept traffic matching the Tailscale mark:
```
meta mark & 0x00040000 == 0x00040000 accept
```

The cfg.tailscale options (hostname, loginServer, sshAuthorizedKeys, exitNode) are already configured. This is just the nftables exemption that's missing.

File: modules/openclaw-vm/default.nix (credproxy-lockdown chain, ~line 569)
close_reason: Implemented by wave 2 workers

title: CODE-REVIEW-3: slice-A (nix-openclaw-vm-1fq)
description:
## Review of Slice A by Reviewer 3 (Elegance + Complexity)

### Requirements Alignment
Faithful to plan 6ce. All OIDC and KV v2 provisioning steps present.

### Elegance and Complexity Matching

#### What works well
- mkIf cfg.devMode.enable at top level is clean gating
- mkMerge approach for adding After/Requires to credproxy is the correct Nix pattern (not mkForce)
- effectiveOidcIssuerURL / effectiveVaultAddress locals cleanly separate concerns
- lib.optionalAttrs for vault.token addition is idiomatic
- Provisioning script follows plan exactly with clear section comments

#### Minor issue: provision service hardening
credproxy-openbao-provision has User/Group = credproxy but no explicit ReadWritePaths or ProtectSystem. Since this service writes /var/lib/credproxy/oidc-client.env, omitting ProtectSystem='strict' is acceptable (it needs to write state), but adding ReadWritePaths = ['/var/lib/credproxy'] and ProtectSystem='strict' would improve the security posture.

#### Minor issue: health poll duration
30 iterations × 1s sleep = 30s max wait. If the host is under load (e.g., building a large Nix derivation), 30s may not be enough for OpenBao to initialize. The Slice B equivalent uses 60 × 2s = 120s. Consistent 60 × 2s across both services would be clearer.

#### Non-issue: static OIDC scope template
Template '{'realm_access':{'roles':['credproxy-user']}}' is static JSON. For MVP with one entity this is correct. Noted in plan as Keycloak-compatible. No over-engineering concern.

### MVP Scope
Appropriate. Single binary, single provisioning script, no new Go files. ✅

### Codebase Quality
No TODO placeholders ✅. Style consistent with existing modules ✅.

## VOTE: REVISE
Reason: The audience mismatch issue (see review-1) is also an elegance problem — the design requires a runtime feedback loop (dynamic client_id → static config) that is not represented in the Nix module structure. The fix could be elegant: pre-set a static client_id in the provisioning script that matches the NixOS oidcAudience default, making the system fully deterministic. Alternatively add a devMode-specific effectiveOidcAudience that reads from the generated file at service start. Either is simpler than the current broken state.
close_reason: Review findings addressed in commit ef84e6d on epic/t5b. All critical and minor items resolved. nix flake check passes.

comments.author: David Huu Pham
comments.text: RESOLVED: ef84e6d addresses all three items: (1) static client_id makes design deterministic; (2) provision service now has ProtectSystem=strict + ReadWritePaths; (3) health poll is 60x2s. VOTE: APPROVE

title: Fix OPA identity.roles path mismatch with Keycloak JWT
description: Rego policy checks input.identity.roles but Keycloak JWTs have roles at realm_access.roles. handlers.go passes RawClaims (which has Keycloak structure), but Rego expects top-level roles. Tests mask this by constructing identity with top-level roles key. Will deny all requests in production. Fix: either transform RawClaims before passing to OPA (inject Roles from AgentIdentity), or update Rego to read input.identity.realm_access.roles.
close_reason: Closed

title: REVIEW W2-3: Elegance and complexity review of wave 2 slices
description:
## Review Axis: Elegance and complexity matching

Review ALL wave 2 slices for proportional complexity:

### General criteria
- Design the API you KNOW you will need (complete for known use cases)
- Do not over-engineer (problem with 3 parts → ~3 part solution)
- Do not under-engineer (genuinely complex problems deserve matching complexity)
- Complexity proportional to innate problem complexity, not code volume

### Slice 0 (h80): goleak + race detector
- Are goleak ignores a temporary workaround or a permanent design smell?
- Is checkPhase the right place for -race, or should it be a separate CI step?

### Slice 1 (glo): Decision pipeline hardening
- Is the select+timeout approach the right abstraction, or should DecisionCh be redesigned entirely (e.g., context-based cancellation)?
- Is removing ValidateIdentity activity the right call, or does it lose Temporal retry benefits for JWKS failures?
- Are the error types (DenialReason enum) complete for known use cases, or will new denial reasons require iota changes?
- Is the roundtrip test appropriately scoped (not too broad, not too narrow)?

### Slice 2 (5rc): TTL eviction
- Is the sweeper goroutine the right pattern, or would time.AfterFunc per-entry be simpler (like connTokens)?
- Is the sweep interval configurable? Should it be?
- Is the TTL default (120s = 2x signal timeout) well-justified?

### Slice 3 (m1b): nftables
- Is interface-based (`oifname "tailscale0"`) the right approach vs mark-based?
- Is the rule hardcoded to "tailscale0" or configurable via NixOS options?
- Is conditional on cfg.tailscale.enable sufficient, or should there be a more explicit option?

### Slice 4 (3mu): TLA+ model
- Is the model at the right abstraction level (not too detailed, not too abstract)?
- Are the invariants the ones that matter most for this system?
- Is the model maintainable as the protocol evolves?

### Cross-slice elegance
- Is the overall architecture (5 slices touching 5 concerns) proportional to the problem?
- Are there abstractions that should be shared across slices but aren't?
- Are there premature abstractions that add complexity without value?
close_reason: Wave 2 reviews completed, findings captured as ruw/d5c/3f9

title: [SLICE-A] OpenBao Dev Mode + Full Provisioning (OIDC + KV v2)
description:
## Production Code Path

**End user runs:** `systemctl start credproxy-openbao-dev && systemctl start credproxy-openbao-provision`

**Systemd lifecycle:** openbao-dev starts → provision oneshot runs → credproxy service starts (with devMode After/Requires)

## Worker Owns (Full Vertical Slice)

Plan backwards from production code path:
1. End: credproxy service starts with correct OIDC/vault config auto-injected
2. Back: credproxy-openbao-provision oneshot runs OIDC + KV v2 provisioning
3. Back: credproxy-openbao-dev systemd service starts OpenBao dev mode
4. Back: devMode NixOS options defined in default.nix

## Files You Own

- `modules/credential-proxy/default.nix` — ALL modifications

## Implementation Details

### 1. Add devMode options (Layer 1: Types)

```nix
devMode = {
  enable = mkEnableOption "Dev mode with OpenBao dev server for auth + secrets";
  openbaoToken = mkOption { type = types.str; default = "dev-token"; };
  openbaoPort = mkOption { type = types.port; default = 8200; };
};
```

### 2. Add credproxy-openbao-dev systemd service

```nix
systemd.services.credproxy-openbao-dev = mkIf cfg.devMode.enable {
  description = "OpenBao Dev Server for Credential Proxy";
  wantedBy = [ "multi-user.target" ];
  serviceConfig = {
    Type = "simple";
    ExecStart = "${pkgs.openbao}/bin/bao server -dev -dev-root-token-id=${cfg.devMode.openbaoToken} -dev-listen-address=127.0.0.1:${toString cfg.devMode.openbaoPort}";
    Restart = "always";
    # Hardening (match credproxy-temporal pattern)
  };
};
```

### 3. Add credproxy-openbao-provision oneshot service

Provisioning script content (bash, from plan 6ce):

**OIDC section (covers opa):**
1. Configure OIDC issuer
2. Create signing key (credproxy-key, 24h rotation)
3. Create identity entity for VM agent
4. Create OIDC assignment
5. Create OIDC scope with realm_access.roles template (Keycloak-compatible claims)
6. Create confidential OIDC client (client_credentials grant)
7. Create OIDC provider
8. Write client credentials to file for guest provisioning

**KV v2 section (covers 8y4):**
1. Enable KV v2 at secret/ (idempotent)
2. Write sample credential: secret/openclaw/credentials/httpbin

Full script is in plan 6ce — reference it directly.

### 4. Auto-config wiring when devMode enabled

When `cfg.devMode.enable`:
- Override oidcIssuerURL to `"http://127.0.0.1:${toString port}/v1/identity/oidc/provider/credproxy"`
- Override vaultAddress to `"http://127.0.0.1:${toString port}"`
- Add vault.token to configFile
- Add After/Requires on credproxy-openbao-provision.service to credproxy service

### 5. Write client credentials file

The provisioning script writes `/var/lib/credproxy/oidc-client.env` containing:
```
CREDPROXY_CLIENT_ID=<generated>
CREDPROXY_CLIENT_SECRET=<generated>
```
This file is consumed by Slice B (guest wiring via fw_cfg).

## Key Design Decisions

- Use `mkIf cfg.devMode.enable` for all new services (gated on devMode)
- Use `mkMerge` to optionally add After/Requires to existing credproxy service
- The provisioning script must wait for OpenBao health check before proceeding
- Use `pkgs.openbao` (requires nixos-unstable with OpenBao 2.5.0)
- Client credentials file has mode 0600 (credproxy user only)
- Use idempotent commands where possible (e.g., `bao secrets enable ... || true`)

## Validation Checklist

Before marking complete:
- [ ] devMode options defined and typed correctly
- [ ] credproxy-openbao-dev service starts OpenBao in dev mode
- [ ] credproxy-openbao-provision runs OIDC + KV v2 setup after dev server healthy
- [ ] OIDC discovery endpoint responds (GET .well-known/openid-configuration)
- [ ] KV v2 secret readable at expected path
- [ ] credproxy service After/Requires updated when devMode enabled
- [ ] configFile auto-includes correct OIDC issuer URL and vault address
- [ ] Client credentials written to /var/lib/credproxy/oidc-client.env
- [ ] nix flake check passes
- [ ] No TODO placeholders in NixOS module code
design:
{
    "productionCodePath": "systemctl start credproxy-openbao-dev && systemctl start credproxy-openbao-provision",
    "validation_checklist": [
      "nix flake check passes",
      "devMode options defined and typed correctly",
      "credproxy-openbao-dev service starts OpenBao dev mode",
      "credproxy-openbao-provision runs OIDC + KV v2 provisioning",
      "configFile auto-includes OIDC issuer URL and vault address when devMode enabled",
      "Client credentials written to /var/lib/credproxy/oidc-client.env",
      "No TODO placeholders"
    ],
    "acceptance_criteria": [
      {
        "given": "devMode.enable = true in NixOS config",
        "when": "system activates",
        "then": "credproxy-openbao-dev starts OpenBao, provision oneshot configures OIDC + KV v2, credproxy gets correct config",
        "should_not": "start credproxy before provisioning completes"
      },
      {
        "given": "provisioning oneshot has run",
        "when": "OIDC discovery endpoint is queried",
        "then": "returns valid OpenID Connect configuration",
        "should_not": "fail with 404 or malformed response"
      },
      {
        "given": "provisioning oneshot has run",
        "when": "KV v2 secret is read",
        "then": "returns CredentialValue with key, header_name, header_prefix",
        "should_not": "return ErrSecretNotFound"
      }
    ],
    "ratified_plan": "6ce",
    "covers_tasks": ["opa", "8y4"]
  }

title: IMPL REVIEW: Credential proxy implementation plan review
description:
# Implementation Plan Review — Tracking Issue

Three independent reviews of the credential-proxy implementation plan (nix-openclaw-vm-7xk) were conducted against three axes:

1. **Correctness** (spirit and technicality to proposal/URD)
2. **Test quality** (integration coverage, mock discipline, fixtures)
3. **Elegance and complexity matching** (interface completeness, over/under-engineering)

## Combined Top Findings

| # | Finding | Source | Severity |
|---|---------|--------|----------|
| 1 | `vendorHash = null` breaks Nix build | Correctness | Blocking |
| 2 | Rego policies not bundled in Nix package | Correctness | Blocking |
| 3 | OPA `roles` path mismatch with Keycloak JWT | Correctness | Likely bug |
| 4 | `ProxyRequestWorkflow` + `FetchAndForward` are dead code (~600 LOC) | Correctness + Elegance | Significant |
| 5 | No body size limits — OOM risk | Elegance | High |
| 6 | Compressed responses bypass scrubbing | Elegance | High |
| 7 | No vault health check at startup | Elegance | Moderate |
| 8 | No workflow tests (testsuite.TestWorkflowEnvironment) | Tests | High |
| 9 | No HTTPS CONNECT integration test | Tests | High |
| 10 | Mock types duplicated across packages | Tests | Medium |
| 11 | Phase 5 network lockdown not implemented | Correctness | Security gap |
| 12 | fw_cfg provisioning not implemented (should source env vars) | Correctness | Missing feature |
| 13 | No VM testing patterns documented in README/AGENTS | All | Documentation gap |

## Disposition

This tracking issue is blocked by the three individual review issues, which are in turn blocked by leaf fix tasks.
close_reason: All review findings fixed: registry.Start wired, dead code removed, scrubCount accuracy fixed, 3 test gaps filled

title: Enable -race flag for Go tests in CI and dev workflow
description:
The RequestRegistry uses sync.Map + channels shared between goproxy goroutines and Temporal local activities. The race detector catches data races at runtime.

Changes:
1. Add `-race` to `go test` invocations in flake.nix checkPhase
2. Document `go test -race ./...` in AGENTS.md test section
3. Ensure CI runs tests with -race enabled

Note: -race adds ~2-10x overhead so may want a separate CI step for race-enabled tests.
close_reason: Implemented by wave 2 workers

title: Study OpenBao KV v2 API and policy model for credential proxy
description: Study OpenBao (Vault-compatible) to understand how credential-proxy will store and retrieve secrets. Focus areas: KV v2 secret engine (data envelope structure), policy language (path-based ACLs for read-only proxy access), AppRole auth method for service authentication, token renewal/revocation, secret versioning, and NixOS module integration (openbao service, policy provisioning).
notes: Research completed. Report written to docs/research/openbao.Rmd. Key findings: KV v2 data.data envelope confirmed working; typed KVv2 API available for upgrade; AppRole auth with periodic tokens recommended for production; LifetimeWatcher provides automatic renewal with backoff; audit logs HMAC-hash all secret values; Transit engine available for future caching; NixOS policy module already provisions credproxy-readonly; OpenBao API endpoints identical to Vault.
close_reason: Research completed, reports written to docs/research/

title: PROPOSAL: Credential proxy implementation plan
description:
# Credential Proxy Security Layer — Implementation Plan

**Beads**: `nix-openclaw-vm-7xk` (epic), `nix-openclaw-vm-et5` (request), `nix-openclaw-vm-whp` (URE)

---

## Research Findings

### Existing Solutions Evaluated

| Solution | What It Does | Domain Binding | VSOCK | Verdict |
|----------|-------------|----------------|-------|---------|
| [Vultrino](https://github.com/zachyking/vultrino) | Rust credential proxy for AI agents. Alias-based refs, MCP server, WASM plugins. 6 stars, Jan 2026. | No (glob scoping only) | No | **Design reference only**. Too immature, Rust (rejected), no domain binding. |
| [Agent Gateway](https://github.com/agentgateway/agentgateway) | Rust MCP/A2A gateway. Linux Foundation. RBAC, JWT, header manipulation. | No | No | **Wrong purpose**. Inbound agent-to-tool routing, NOT outbound credential injection. |
| [elazarl/goproxy](https://github.com/elazarl/goproxy) | Go HTTP proxy library. MITM HTTPS, per-host handlers, request/response modification. 6.6k stars, BSD-3, 10+ years. | N/A (library) | Yes (custom `net.Listener`) | **Foundation library**. Implements `http.Handler` — serves on any listener including VSOCK. |
| Envoy + ext_authz | C++ L7 proxy (CNCF). ext_authz calls external service for auth + header injection. In nixpkgs. | Header-only (ext_authz can't modify bodies) | No (TCP/Unix only) | **Wrong tool.** ext_authz injects headers but cannot modify request/response bodies. MITM HTTPS is experimental (no cert generation). No VSOCK. Would still need custom Go for body substitution, response scrubbing, Temporal, OpenBao — making Envoy pure overhead (3 moving parts instead of 1). |
| Vault/OpenBao Agent | Proxies requests TO vault, not outbound. | N/A | No | **Backend only**. Use OpenBao for secret storage. |
| [Secretless Broker](https://github.com/cyberark/secretless-broker) | Go HTTP forward proxy by CyberArk. Injects credentials from Vault/Conjur into outbound HTTP. Plugin arch (Providers + Connectors). Apache 2.0, 366 stars, v1.7.32 Feb 2026, maintained since 2018. 25+ pre-built API configs. | URL regex matching, per-connector-port | No (tcp/unix only) | **Closest existing solution but 3 blockers**: (1) Rejects CONNECT with 405 — cannot MITM HTTPS; (2) No response sanitization — "app never sees secrets" model conflicts with our need to scrub credentials from API responses; (3) No VSOCK or Temporal. |
| [Octelium](https://github.com/octelium/octelium) | Go zero-trust access platform. L7-aware "secretless access" — injects API keys, Bearer, OAuth2, Sigv4 per-upstream. CEL/OPA policy-as-code. 3.4k stars, AGPLv3. | Per-service routing + CEL policies | No | **Too heavyweight + AGPLv3**. Full platform (VPN, ZTNA, PaaS, MCP gateway). No response sanitization documented. No VSOCK. |
| [Envoy credential_injector](https://www.envoyproxy.io/docs/envoy/latest/configuration/http/http_filters/credential_injector_filter) | Native Envoy filter. Injects credentials from SDS into outbound requests. First-class in Envoy Gateway v1.4+. | Via routing rules / HTTPRouteFilter | No | **Kubernetes-centric, heavyweight**. Requires full Envoy. No response sanitization. |
| [Ory Oathkeeper](https://www.ory.com/docs/oathkeeper) | Go zero-trust proxy. Per-domain access rules, header/JWT injection, CNCF ecosystem. | Per-rule domain matching | No | **Inbound-focused**. Designed for authenticating users TO your services, not injecting creds into outbound API calls. |
| [Peta Core](https://github.com/dunialabs/peta-core) | TypeScript MCP gateway. Server-side vault with JIT decryption (30s TTL), short-lived agent tokens, HITL approvals. 28 stars, ELv2. | No | No | **TypeScript (not Go), ELv2 license**. Custom vault (not OpenBao). No domain binding. Design reference for JIT decryption + audit patterns. |
| [Riptides](https://riptides.io/) | **Proprietary** kernel module for on-the-wire credential injection. kTLS + SPIFFE identity. Go userspace agent for OPA policy evaluation. Intercepts outbound IP sockets before TLS. | Per-process SPIFFE scoping | No (IP sockets only, not AF_VSOCK) | **Proprietary, no VSOCK, no response sanitization.** Core kernel module is closed-source. Plans to open-source "the core" but hasn't. Uses Lima VMs (TAP networking) — likely incompatible with VSOCK-only microVMs. No response scrubbing. No Temporal. Design reference for on-the-wire injection timing + SPIFFE identity. |
| [Boundary](https://developer.hashicorp.com/boundary) (HashiCorp) | Credential brokering + injection for SSH, RDP, databases. Controller+Worker model. BSL 1.1. | Per-target | No | **3 blockers**: (1) BSL license (not truly OSS since 2023); (2) Credential injection is **Enterprise/HCP-only** — community edition only has brokering; (3) HTTP API injection is [not implemented](https://github.com/hashicorp/boundary/issues/5273) — only SSH, RDP, DB targets supported. |
| [Pomerium](https://www.pomerium.com/) | Zero-trust access proxy. JWT injection + Vault integration. Apache 2.0. | Per-route | No | **Inbound-focused + JWT only.** Authenticates users TO apps. Can inject JWTs but not arbitrary API keys/headers into outbound requests. |
| [Teleport](https://goteleport.com/) | Secure access platform. REST API access with JWT injection. Elastic v2/AGPL. | Per-app | No | **Restrictive license + JWT only.** Elastic v2 is not OSS. Same JWT-only limitation as Pomerium. |

**Conclusion**: No existing solution provides our required combination (VSOCK + MITM HTTPS + placeholder substitution + response sanitization + domain binding + Temporal audit). CyberArk Secretless Broker was closest but has fundamental conflicts with response sanitization (its "app never sees secrets" model means it can't scrub credentials from API responses). Build custom Go proxy using [goproxy](https://github.com/elazarl/goproxy) as the HTTP proxy foundation and [mdlayher/vsock](https://github.com/mdlayher/vsock) as the transport. Borrow design patterns from Secretless (Vault provider, connector configs) and Peta (JIT decryption, audit trail) without taking the dependencies.

### Go Libraries

| Library | Component | Purpose | Status |
|---------|-----------|---------|--------|
| [`elazarl/goproxy`](https://github.com/elazarl/goproxy) | (4)(5) Gateway | HTTP/HTTPS forward proxy with MITM | 6.6k stars, BSD-3, 10+ years |
| [`mdlayher/vsock`](https://github.com/mdlayher/vsock) | (4) Transport | AF_VSOCK `net.Listener` | v1 stable, updated Jan 2025 |
| [`coreos/go-oidc/v3`](https://github.com/coreos/go-oidc) | (2) Authentication | Keycloak JWT validation via JWKS | Industry standard |
| [`open-policy-agent/opa/rego`](https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/open-policy-agent/opa/rego) | (3) Authorization | Rego policy evaluation (embedded) | CNCF graduated, <10ms P99 |
| [`openbao/openbao/api/v2`](https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/openbao/openbao/api) | (1) Secret vault | OpenBao client (Vault API compat) | Official, maintained |
| [`temporalio/sdk-go`](https://go.temporal.io/sdk) | Full lifecycle orchestration | Workflows, activities, signals, search attributes | Tier 1, v1.29+, production-grade |
| `temporal-cli` | Infrastructure | Temporal dev server | [In nixpkgs](https://www.nixhub.io/packages/temporal-cli) |

### Auth/Authz Tool Evaluation

Pomerium, Ory Oathkeeper, and OPA were evaluated for components (2) and (3):

| Tool | Keycloak OIDC | Embeddable | Forward proxy (CONNECT) | Authz model | Verdict |
|------|--------------|------------|------------------------|-------------|---------|
| **Pomerium** | Native (redirect flow) | **No** — standalone only | Partial (needs pomerium-cli) | PPL (YAML) + Rego | **Too heavy**. Can't embed, requires separate process. |
| **Oathkeeper** | JWT validation | **Yes** (gRPC middleware) | **No** | Rules + remote authorizer | **No CONNECT**. Good mutators but wrong proxy mode. |
| **OPA (embedded)** | JWT decode in Rego | **Yes** (library) | N/A (policy engine) | Rego (very flexible) | **Winner for (3).** <10ms, embeddable, expressive. |
| **coreos/go-oidc** | JWKS validation | **Yes** (library) | N/A | N/A | **Winner for (2).** Simple, 5 lines of code. |

**Decision:** Use `coreos/go-oidc` for JWT validation + `OPA embedded` for authorization policy. Skip Pomerium and Oathkeeper — they don't handle CONNECT, and their auth can be done more simply with direct libraries.

### Language Elimination

| Language | Temporal SDK | Vault Client | OIDC | VSOCK | Verdict |
|----------|-------------|-------------|------|-------|---------|
| **Go** | Tier 1 | First-party | Excellent | `mdlayher/vsock` | **Winner** |
| Rust | Alpha | Community | Good | Available | User rejected; Temporal experimental |
| Gleam/Elixir | **None** | Poor | Fair | **None** | Eliminated |
| Zig | **None** | **None** | **None** | Syscall | Eliminated |

## Context

The openclaw-vm gives an AI agent a sandboxed microVM with VSOCK-only host communication. Currently, the agent needs API credentials passed in directly (via fw_cfg), meaning a compromised agent could exfiltrate secrets. This plan builds a **transparent forward proxy** on the host that:

- Listens on **VSOCK** (hypervisor-level socket — physically cannot reach the internet, no IP routing)
- Acts as a standard HTTP forward proxy — agent just sets `HTTP_PROXY` / `HTTPS_PROXY` env vars
- **Intercepts HTTPS** via MITM TLS with a custom CA cert installed in the VM
- Detects **opaque placeholder strings** (`agent-vault-<uuid>`) in request headers/body
- **Replaces placeholders with real credentials** fetched from OpenBao
- Enforces **domain binding** (credential X only works for domain Y, fail-closed)
- **Scrubs credentials from responses** (replaces real values back with placeholders)
- Records every credential access as a **Temporal workflow** for audit/replay

**Why VSOCK?** Unlike TAP networking (which relies on nftables rules that could be misconfigured), VSOCK is a direct guest↔host channel provided by the hypervisor. Traffic on VSOCK physically cannot be routed to the internet — it's not an IP socket. This provides defense-in-depth: even if firewall rules are wrong, credentials can only flow through the proxy.

## Key Decisions

| Decision | Choice | Rationale |
|----------|--------|-----------|
| Language | **Go** | First-party Vault client, tier-1 Temporal SDK, goproxy library, VSOCK support |
| Architecture | **Composable 5-component gateway** | Separation of concerns: auth, authz, vault, inbound, outbound. Single Go binary. |
| Proxy foundation | **goproxy, not Secretless Broker** | Secretless rejects CONNECT (405) — no MITM HTTPS. goproxy handles MITM natively. |
| Authentication | **Keycloak OIDC JWT via `coreos/go-oidc`** | Agent presents JWT in `Proxy-Authorization` header. Proxy validates against Keycloak JWKS. Pomerium/Oathkeeper evaluated but too heavy (can't embed, wrong proxy mode). |
| Authorization | **OPA embedded (Rego policies)** | Evaluates "identity X with role Y can use credential Z for domain D". <10ms P99, embeddable library. |
| Proxy mode | **Transparent forward proxy** (goproxy) | Agent sets `HTTP_PROXY` env var. Zero agent code changes. |
| HTTPS handling | **MITM with custom CA** | goproxy's `ConnectMitm` action. CA cert installed in VM at build time. |
| Credential pattern | **Placeholder substitution** | `agent-vault-<uuid>` strings in headers/body. Proxy replaces outbound, scrubs inbound. |
| Orchestration | **Temporal as access decision orchestrator** | Every proxy access attempt = `ProxyAccessDecision` workflow. Authn → authz → credential path resolution are activities. goproxy owns HTTP lifecycle; Temporal owns the decision pipeline. Audit trail, replay, retries, rate limiting. See [temporal-agent-orchestration.Rmd](docs/research/temporal-agent-orchestration.Rmd). |
| Provisioning | **Env vars + fw_cfg** | `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=agent-vault-abc123` + JSON config listing all placeholders. |
| Deployment | **Systemd service** on host | Direct VSOCK access, systemd hardening. |
| Temporal hosting | **temporal-cli dev server** | In nixpkgs. SQLite-backed. Upgrade to PostgreSQL later. |
| Domain enforcement | **OPA policy + goproxy `DstHostIs()`** | OPA for fine-grained authz, goproxy for fail-closed domain allowlist. |

## Architecture (Temporal-Orchestrated Access Decision Gateway)

goproxy owns the HTTP lifecycle (CONNECT, MITM TLS, request/response forwarding).
Temporal owns the access decision pipeline (authn, authz, credential path resolution).
Secret values never enter Temporal event history — only credential *paths* flow through workflows.

## Request Flow (Temporal Full-Lifecycle Orchestration)

```
1. Agent authenticates to Keycloak, receives JWT.
   Agent makes normal HTTPS request with JWT:
     curl -H "x-api-key: agent-vault-abc123" \
          -H "Proxy-Authorization: Bearer <jwt>" \
          https://api.anthropic.com/v1/messages

2. curl respects HTTP_PROXY=http://localhost:18790
   → Sends CONNECT api.anthropic.com:443 to proxy (with Proxy-Authorization header)

3. socat in VM bridges localhost:18790 → VSOCK CID:4 port:18790 → Host

4. GOPROXY HandleConnect:
   a. Extract Proxy-Authorization JWT, store in connTokens
   b. Is api.anthropic.com in domain allowlist?
      NO  → ConnectReject
      YES → ConnectMitm (MITM intercept with custom CA)

5. goproxy decrypts the HTTPS request via MITM TLS.

6. GOPROXY OnRequest handler:
   a. Retrieve JWT from connTokens
   b. Extract placeholders from headers + body (regex: agent-vault-[a-f0-9-]{36})
   c. If no placeholders → pass through (no workflow needed)
   d. Register RequestContext in RequestRegistry (request ID → *http.Request + chan)
   e. Start Temporal workflow: ProxyRequestWorkflow

7. TEMPORAL WORKFLOW: ProxyRequestWorkflow
   Input: { requestID, rawJWT, placeholders[], targetDomain }
   Search attributes set immediately: TargetDomain, Status=in_progress

   7a. Activity: ValidateIdentity (regular — JWKS needs network)
       - Verify JWT signature, issuer, audience, expiry via Keycloak JWKS
       - Extract claims: { sub, roles, groups }
       - Returns: IdentityClaims (safe for event history)
       INVALID → workflow finalizes as denied, sends denial on DecisionCh

   7b. Local Activity: EvaluatePolicy (OPA is in-process)
       - Input: { identity claims, placeholders, target_domain }
       - Resolves credential bindings from config
       - Evaluates Rego policy: identity.roles → credential → domain
       DENIED → workflow finalizes as denied, sends denial on DecisionCh

   7c. Local Activity: FetchAndInject (vault fetch + request modification)
       - Looks up RequestContext from RequestRegistry by requestID
       - Resolves vault paths from config
       - Fetches real credentials from OpenBao
       - Replaces placeholders in *http.Request headers/body (in-place)
       - Populates RequestContext.ScrubMap (realValue → placeholder)
       - Sends "allowed" decision on DecisionCh (unblocks goproxy)
       - Returns: InjectResult { credentialCount } (no secrets in output)

   7d. Workflow waits for "response_complete" signal (up to 60s timeout)

8. GOPROXY (unblocked by DecisionCh):
   a. Request was already modified in-place by FetchAndInject
   b. Forward modified request to api.anthropic.com
   c. Receive response

9. GOPROXY OnResponse handler:
   a. Scan response body for real credential values (using ScrubMap from registry)
   b. Replace any found values with their placeholder strings
   c. Clean up connTokens entry
   d. Signal workflow: "response_complete" with { statusCode, scrubCount, bytes }
   e. Return sanitized response through MITM TLS → VSOCK → Agent

10. TEMPORAL WORKFLOW receives signal, updates search attributes, completes.
    Full audit trail: agentID, targetDomain, credentialCount, scrubCount,
    statusCode, latencyMs, allowed/denied status — all queryable via
    Temporal's search attribute API.
```

## goproxy Integration Details

### Responsibility Split: goproxy vs Temporal

| Concern | Owner | Rationale |
|---------|-------|-----------|
| CONNECT handling, domain allowlist | goproxy | Must happen before MITM; no benefit from durability |
| JWT extraction from headers | goproxy | Parsing only — deterministic, no I/O |
| Placeholder extraction from body/headers | goproxy | Parsing only — deterministic, no I/O |
| JWT verification (JWKS) | Temporal activity | Network I/O (JWKS fetch), benefits from retry |
| OPA policy evaluation | Temporal activity | May timeout, benefits from retry + audit |
| Credential path resolution | Temporal activity | Config lookup with audit trail |
| Vault secret fetch | Temporal local activity (`FetchAndInject`) | In-process via RequestRegistry; secrets never serialized |
| Placeholder→credential substitution | Temporal local activity (`FetchAndInject`) | Modifies `*http.Request` in-place via RequestRegistry |
| HTTP forwarding | goproxy | Owns the HTTP lifecycle |
| Response credential scrubbing | goproxy + Temporal signal | goproxy scrubs; signals workflow with outcome metadata |
| Audit recording | Temporal (workflow lifecycle + search attributes) | Full lifecycle: authn, authz, inject, forward, scrub |

### Handler Registration

```go
// HandleConnect: domain allowlist + JWT extraction (no Temporal yet)
proxy.OnRequest().HandleConnect(goproxy.FuncHttpsHandler(
    func(host string, ctx *goproxy.ProxyCtx) (*goproxy.ConnectAction, string) {
        domain := stripPort(host)
        if !gw.cfg.IsAllowedDomain(domain) {
            return goproxy.RejectConnect, host
        }
        gw.connTokens.Store(ctx.Req.RemoteAddr, extractBearerToken(
            ctx.Req.Header.Get("Proxy-Authorization")))
        return goproxy.MitmConnect, host
    }))

// OnRequest: register context → start Temporal workflow → block on decision
proxy.OnRequest().DoFunc(func(req *http.Request, ctx *goproxy.ProxyCtx) (*http.Request, *http.Response) {
    rawToken := gw.resolveToken(req)
    if rawToken == "" {
        return req, errorResponse(req, 407, "missing authentication token")
    }
    req.Header.Del("Proxy-Authorization")

    placeholders, _ := Extract(req)
    if len(placeholders) == 0 {
        return req, nil // No credentials needed — pass through
    }

    // Register request in shared registry for local activities
    requestID := generateRequestID()
    decisionCh := make(chan *WorkflowDecision, 1)
    reqCtx := &RequestContext{Request: req, DecisionCh: decisionCh}
    gw.registry.Store(requestID, reqCtx)
    defer gw.registry.Delete(requestID)

    // Start Temporal workflow (stays alive until response_complete signal)
    targetDomain := stripPort(req.Host)
    run, err := gw.temporal.ExecuteWorkflow(bgCtx, client.StartWorkflowOptions{
        ID:        fmt.Sprintf("proxy-%s-%s", targetDomain, requestID),
        TaskQueue: gw.cfg.Temporal.TaskQueue,
    }, workflows.ProxyRequestWorkflow, workflows.ProxyInput{
        RequestID:    requestID,
        RawJWT:       rawToken,
        Placeholders: placeholders,
        TargetDomain: targetDomain,
    })

    // Block until FetchAndInject activity signals decision (NOT run.Get())
    decision := <-decisionCh
    if decision.Status != StatusAllowed {
        return req, errorResponse(req, 403, decision.Reason)
    }

    // Request was modified in-place by FetchAndInject. ScrubMap populated.
    ctx.UserData = &requestState{
        scrubMap:   reqCtx.ScrubMap,
        workflowID: run.GetID(),
        runID:      run.GetRunID(),
    }
    return req, nil
})

// OnResponse: scrub credentials, then signal workflow completion
proxy.OnResponse().DoFunc(func(resp *http.Response, ctx *goproxy.ProxyCtx) *http.Response {
    state := ctx.UserData.(*requestState)
    scrubCount := ScrubCredentials(resp, state.scrubMap)

    // Signal workflow that response is handled — completes the audit trail
    gw.temporal.SignalWorkflow(bgCtx, state.workflowID, state.runID,
        "response_complete", ResponseOutcome{
            StatusCode:       resp.StatusCode,
            ScrubCount:       scrubCount,
            BytesTransferred: resp.ContentLength,
        })

    return resp
})
```

### Custom CA Certificate

Generated at NixOS build time:
```nix
# In modules/credential-proxy/default.nix
pkgs.runCommand "credproxy-ca" { buildInputs = [ pkgs.openssl ]; } ''
  openssl req -x509 -newkey ec -pkeyopt ec_paramgen_curve:prime256v1 \
    -keyout $out/ca.key -out $out/ca.crt -days 3650 -nodes \
    -subj '/CN=Credential Proxy CA/O=openclaw-vm'
''
```

Installed in guest VM:
```nix
# In guest.nix
security.pki.certificateFiles = [ "${credproxyCa}/ca.crt" ];
```

## Temporal Workflow Design

### ProxyRequest Workflow — Full Lifecycle Orchestration

The workflow orchestrates the **entire proxy request lifecycle** — authentication,
authorization, credential injection, and response scrubbing audit. It stays alive for
the full HTTP request duration, receiving a completion signal from goproxy when the
response has been scrubbed.

**Key constraint**: goproxy owns the live `*http.Request` and `*http.Response` objects
(they cannot be serialized into Temporal). Solution: a `RequestRegistry` (`sync.Map`)
shared between goproxy and Temporal local activities. Activities reference requests by
ID; secrets flow through in-process memory, never through Temporal event history.

```
Input:  { requestID, rawJWT, placeholders[], targetDomain }
Output: { status, statusCode, credentialCount, scrubCount, latencyMs }

Search attributes: AgentID, TargetDomain, CredentialRefHash, Status

Signals:
  "response_complete" → { statusCode, scrubCount, bytesTransferred }
```

```go
func ProxyRequestWorkflow(ctx workflow.Context, input ProxyInput) (*ProxyOutput, error) {
    start := workflow.Now(ctx)
    upsertSearchAttributes(ctx, "", input.TargetDomain, input.Placeholders, StatusInProgress)

    // Phase 1: Validate identity (regular activity — JWKS may need network)
    var identity IdentityClaims
    err := workflow.ExecuteActivity(authnCtx, ValidateIdentity, input.RawJWT).Get(ctx, &identity)
    if err != nil {
        return finalize(ctx, start, StatusDenied, "authentication_failed")
    }
    upsertSearchAttributes(ctx, identity.Subject, "", nil, "")

    // Phase 2: Evaluate authorization policy (local activity — OPA is in-process)
    var decision AuthzDecision
    err = workflow.ExecuteLocalActivity(authzCtx, EvaluatePolicy, AuthzInput{
        Identity: identity, Placeholders: input.Placeholders, TargetDomain: input.TargetDomain,
    }).Get(ctx, &decision)
    if err != nil || !decision.Allowed {
        return finalize(ctx, start, StatusDenied, decision.Reason)
    }

    // Phase 3: Fetch credentials from vault and inject into request (local activity)
    // Accesses *http.Request via RequestRegistry[input.RequestID].
    // Secrets stay in-process — only metadata in event history.
    var injectResult InjectResult
    err = workflow.ExecuteLocalActivity(injectCtx, FetchAndInject, FetchAndInjectInput{
        RequestID:    input.RequestID,
        Placeholders: input.Placeholders,
    }).Get(ctx, &injectResult)
    if err != nil {
        return finalize(ctx, start, StatusError, "credential_injection_failed")
    }

    // Phase 4: Wait for goproxy to forward + scrub response.
    // goproxy signals us when response scrubbing is complete.
    var outcome ResponseOutcome
    timerCtx, _ := workflow.WithDeadline(ctx, workflow.Now(ctx).Add(60*time.Second))
    signalCh := workflow.GetSignalChannel(timerCtx, "response_complete")
    signalCh.Receive(timerCtx, &outcome)

    return &ProxyOutput{
        Status:          StatusSuccess,
        StatusCode:      outcome.StatusCode,
        CredentialCount: injectResult.CredentialCount,
        ScrubCount:      outcome.ScrubCount,
        LatencyMs:       workflow.Now(ctx).Sub(start).Milliseconds(),
    }, nil
}
```

### Request Registry (Shared In-Process State)

goproxy and Temporal local activities run in the same process. The `RequestRegistry`
bridges them without serializing secrets through Temporal:

```go
// In proxy/registry.go
type RequestRegistry struct {
    entries sync.Map // requestID → *RequestContext
}

type RequestContext struct {
    Request    *http.Request            // live HTTP request (goproxy owns this)
    ScrubMap   map[string]string        // realValue → placeholder (populated by FetchAndInject)
    DecisionCh chan<- *WorkflowDecision // unblocks goproxy when injection is complete
}
```

Flow:
1. goproxy creates `RequestContext`, stores in registry, starts workflow
2. goproxy blocks on `DecisionCh` (NOT `run.Get()` — workflow stays alive)
3. Workflow activities access the `RequestContext` by ID
4. `FetchAndInject` activity: fetches secrets from vault, modifies `*http.Request`,
   populates scrub map, sends decision on `DecisionCh`
5. goproxy unblocks, forwards request, scrubs response using `ScrubMap`
6. goproxy signals workflow with `response_complete` (status, scrub count, bytes)
7. Workflow records final audit metadata and completes

### Activities

1. **ValidateIdentity** (regular activity — authn via coreos/go-oidc)
   - Input: raw JWT string
   - Verifies JWT signature against Keycloak JWKS (cached, but may need network)
   - Extracts claims: sub, realm_access.roles, groups
   - Returns: `IdentityClaims` (safe for event history)
   - Retry: 2 attempts (JWKS endpoint may be temporarily unreachable)
   - Timeout: 10s start-to-close

2. **EvaluatePolicy** (local activity — authz via OPA embedded)
   - Input: identity claims + placeholders + target domain
   - Resolves placeholder→credential bindings from config
   - Evaluates Rego policy: "identity.roles → credential → domain"
   - Returns: `AuthzDecision { allowed, reason }`
   - Local activity: OPA evaluation is in-process, no network
   - Timeout: 5s start-to-close

3. **FetchAndInject** (local activity — vault fetch + credential injection)
   - Input: `{ requestID, placeholders }` (no secrets in input)
   - Looks up `RequestContext` from `RequestRegistry` by requestID
   - Resolves credential vault paths from config
   - Fetches real credentials from OpenBao (network call to vault)
   - Replaces placeholders in request headers/body (modifies `*http.Request` in-place)
   - Populates `RequestContext.ScrubMap` for response scrubbing
   - Sends decision on `RequestContext.DecisionCh` (unblocks goproxy)
   - Returns: `InjectResult { credentialCount }` (no secrets in output)
   - Timeout: 30s start-to-close

### Secrets Safety

Secret values NEVER appear in Temporal event history:

| Data | In Temporal history? | Where it lives |
|------|---------------------|----------------|
| Raw JWT | Yes (activity input) | Short-lived, already on VSOCK |
| Identity claims | Yes (activity output) | Public metadata from JWT |
| Credential paths | No (local activity) | In-process config lookup |
| Real secret values | **No** | In-process memory via RequestRegistry |
| Scrub map | **No** | In-process memory via RequestRegistry |
| HTTP request/response | **No** | goproxy memory via RequestRegistry |
| Scrub count / status code | Yes (signal payload) | Metadata, not secrets |

### What This Replaces

The previous implementation had:
- `ProxyRequestWorkflow` — dead code that tried to own the HTTP lifecycle inside a
  `FetchAndForward` activity, rebuilding requests from scratch (discarding headers/body).
- `AuditWorkflow` — fire-and-forget metadata logger, only recorded successes.
- All authn/authz/vault/scrub logic was inline in goproxy `handleRequest`.

The new `ProxyRequestWorkflow` orchestrates the full lifecycle:
- **Authn, authz, credential injection** are Temporal activities
- **Response scrubbing** is tracked via signal from goproxy to the workflow
- Every access attempt (allowed AND denied) is a complete workflow with search attributes
- The workflow is the single source of truth for "what happened with this request"

- `credential-proxy/proxy/handlers.go` — OnRequest (start workflow + block on ch), OnResponse (scrub + signal)
- `credential-proxy/workflows/proxy_workflow.go` — ProxyRequestWorkflow (full lifecycle + signal)
- `credential-proxy/workflows/activities.go` — ValidateIdentity, EvaluatePolicy, FetchAndInject
- `credential-proxy/main.go` — VSOCK listener, goproxy setup, Temporal worker

### Phase 4: NixOS Integration
- `modules/credential-proxy/default.nix` — Host module (proxy + Temporal + CA gen)
- `modules/credential-proxy/guest.nix` — Guest module (VSOCK bridge, CA cert, env vars)
- `modules/credential-proxy/openbao-policy.nix` — Read-only policy
- `flake.nix` — `buildGoModule` package, module imports, test-vm config
- `modules/openclaw-vm/default.nix` — Wire in credentialProxy options, VSOCK port 18790
- `modules/openclaw-vm/guest.nix` — HTTP_PROXY/HTTPS_PROXY, CA cert, placeholder env vars

### Phase 5: VM Network Lockdown
- Extend nftables in `modules/openclaw-vm/default.nix` — block all external network when proxy enabled
- Only VSOCK communication allowed

---

## Verification

1. **Nix evaluation**: `nix flake check --no-build` passes
2. **Module evaluation**: `nix eval .#nixosConfigurations.test-vm.config.system.build.toplevel --apply 'x: "ok"'` → `"ok"`
3. **Go build**: `nix build .#credential-proxy` succeeds
4. **Go tests**: `cd credential-proxy && go test ./...` — all pass
5. **Placeholder detection**: Unit tests verify extraction from HTTP headers, JSON body, URL query params
6. **Sanitization**: Unit tests verify credential values scrubbed from responses (JSON, HTML, plaintext)
7. **Domain binding**: Unit tests verify mismatch → denied (fail-closed), match → allowed
8. **MITM TLS**: Integration test with custom CA cert — proxy decrypts HTTPS, injects credentials
9. **Temporal workflow**: Integration test with `go.temporal.io/sdk/testsuite` — ProxyRequestWorkflow completes full lifecycle (authn → authz → inject → signal → audit)
10. **Forward proxy**: Integration test — `curl -x http://proxy:18790 https://httpbin.org/get` works with placeholder substitution
11. **Network lockdown**: Agent cannot reach external network without proxy (no DNS, no TCP out)

---

## Existing Code to Reuse

| Pattern | Source | Reuse |
|---------|--------|-------|
| VSOCK socat bridge | `modules/openclaw-vm/guest.nix:466-488` (vsock-gateway-proxy) | Same pattern for credproxy VSOCK bridge |
| fw_cfg credential injection | `modules/openclaw-vm/default.nix:397-440` (sops.templates + fw_cfg) | Same pattern for placeholder config |
| systemd hardening | `modules/openclaw-vm/guest.nix:293-370` (openclaw-gateway service) | Copy hardening directives for proxy service |
| nftables VM isolation | `modules/openclaw-vm/default.nix:190-260` (nftables rules) | Extend for full network lockdown |
| OpenBao policy gen | `modules/openclaw/openbao.nix:42-70` (per-instance policies) | Same pattern for proxy read-only policy |
| CA cert install | NixOS `security.pki.certificateFiles` | Standard NixOS pattern for custom CAs |

title: [SLICE 4] TLA+/PlusCal formal verification model
description:
## Vertical Slice: Concurrency Formal Verification

**Production artifact:** TLA+/PlusCal model of the goproxy ↔ Temporal ↔ RequestRegistry concurrency protocol. Model checker verifies no deadlocks, no double-injection, eventual cleanup.

**End user benefit:** Confidence that the concurrency protocol is correct by construction. Catches entire classes of bugs (deadlock, livelock, race conditions) that testing alone cannot prove absent.

### Worker Owns (Full Vertical)

Plan backwards from verification:
1. End: TLC model checker passes all invariants with zero violations
2. Back: Safety + liveness properties defined (NoDeadlock, EveryRequestGetsResponse, RegistryEventuallyEmpty, NoDoubleInjection)
3. Back: PlusCal process definitions for 3 actors
4. Back: State variables matching finalized protocol from Slice 1

### Leaf Tasks

- **xno** (P2): Create TLA+/PlusCal state machine model for proxy concurrency protocol

### DEPENDS ON: Slice 1

This slice MUST wait for Slice 1 (hardened request decision pipeline) to complete. The TLA+ model must reflect the FINALIZED concurrency protocol, including:
- select+timeout on DecisionCh (from ct4)
- JWT validation in goproxy, not workflow (from zyg)
- Typed error flow (from d3k)

### Files You Own

- credential-proxy/model/proxy_protocol.tla (NEW)
- credential-proxy/model/README.md (NEW: how to run TLC)

### Implementation Order

**Layer 1: State Variables**
- RequestRegistry states: empty / has_entry / stale
- DecisionCh states: empty / signaled_allow / signaled_deny
- response_complete signal: pending / delivered
- Workflow states: init / evaluating / injecting / waiting_signal / complete

**Layer 2: Process Definitions (~80-120 lines PlusCal)**
- GoproxyHandler: register → start_workflow → select(decisionCh, timeout) → forward → scrub → signal
- ProxyRequestWorkflow: evaluate_policy → fetch_and_inject → wait_signal → finalize
- FetchAndInjectActivity: registry_lookup → vault_fetch → modify_request → signal_decision

**Layer 3: Invariants + Properties**
- NoDeadlock: no reachable state where all actors blocked
- SecretsNeverInHistory: credential values never in workflow I/O
- EveryRequestGetsResponse: registered request eventually gets decision
- RegistryEventuallyEmpty: all entries eventually cleaned
- NoDoubleInjection: FetchAndInject runs at most once per request

### Validation

- [ ] TLC model checker: zero violations across full state space
- [ ] All 5 invariants verified
- [ ] Model runs in < 30 seconds
- [ ] README documents how to run TLC
close_reason: All leaf tasks completed by wave 2 workers

title: [SLICE 2] Resource lifecycle management: TTL eviction + cleanup
description:
## Vertical Slice: Resource Cleanup Lifecycle

**Production code path:** Long-running proxy under sustained load — RequestRegistry and connTokens entries are bounded by TTL eviction, preventing memory leaks from client disconnects, panics, or timeout edge cases.

**End user observes:** Proxy memory usage stays bounded over hours/days of operation. No gradual memory growth from leaked registry entries or orphaned JWT tokens.

### Worker Owns (Full Vertical)

Plan backwards from production code path:
1. End: Registry entries evicted after 2× signal timeout (~120s), connTokens cleaned after TTL
2. Back: Background sweeper goroutine in RequestRegistry
3. Back: Timestamp tracking on Store(), TTL configuration in config
4. Back: connTokens time.AfterFunc on Store, cancel on Delete

### Leaf Tasks

- **8h6** (P1): Add RequestRegistry TTL-based eviction and connTokens cleanup

### Files You Own

- credential-proxy/proxy/registry.go (TTL tracking, background sweeper, cleanup)
- credential-proxy/proxy/gateway.go (connTokens cleanup, sweeper lifecycle)
- credential-proxy/config/config.go (TTL config options if needed)
- credential-proxy/proxy/registry_test.go (NEW: TTL eviction tests)

### Implementation Order

**Layer 1: Types**
- Add timestamp field to registry entries
- Add TTL config option (default: 120s = 2× signal timeout)

**Layer 2: Tests**
- Test: entry evicted after TTL
- Test: active entry NOT evicted
- Test: connTokens cleaned up on disconnect
- Test: sweeper goroutine exits cleanly on shutdown

**Layer 3: Implementation**
- RequestRegistry: track creation time per entry, background goroutine sweeps every TTL/2
- connTokens: time.AfterFunc per entry, cancel timer on delete
- Sweeper lifecycle: start in NewGateway, stop via context cancellation
- Tests should now PASS

### Validation

- [ ] `go test -race ./proxy/ -v` passes
- [ ] Registry entries evicted after TTL in tests
- [ ] connTokens entries cleaned on timeout
- [ ] Sweeper goroutine exits cleanly (goleak-safe)
- [ ] No memory growth under sustained load simulation

title: BUG: nix build .#test-vm fails — security.pki.certificateFiles references runtime CA cert path
description:
## Symptom

`nix build .#test-vm` fails with:
  FileNotFoundError: Bundle not found: /var/lib/credproxy/ca/ca.crt

## Root Cause

guest.nix passes cfg.caCertFile (= /var/lib/credproxy/ca/ca.crt) to security.pki.certificateFiles.
Nix tries to include this path in the system closure at build time, but the file is only generated
at runtime by the credproxy-ca-init oneshot service. The build host cannot find it.

nix flake check passes because it only evaluates the module tree; nix build actually constructs
the closure and hits this.

## Fix

Generate a fixed CA key + self-signed cert, check the cert into the repo (plaintext, it is a
public cert), encrypt the private key with sops-nix, and deploy both via sops-nix at activation
time. The cert's Nix store path can then be passed to security.pki.certificateFiles at build time.

Each host/consumer of the module needs their own .sops.yaml entry and age key.
Reference implementation: ~/dotfiles/ (sops-nix setup with age keys at /var/lib/sops-nix/keys.txt)

## Blocks

VM cannot be built or booted until resolved.
close_reason: Fixed: devMode now generates CA at Nix build time via pkgs.runCommand. The Nix store cert path is used for security.pki.certificateFiles. All quality gates pass: nix flake check, go test -race, ast-grep, nix build .#test-vm.

comments.author: David Huu Pham

title: Plan: MVP OpenBao-only auth+secrets for credential proxy demo
description:
# MVP Auth + Secrets — Revised Implementation Plan

**Scope**: MVP epic `wj1`. Uses **real OpenBao dev mode** for both credential storage (KV v2) and OIDC JWT issuance (identity/oidc provider). Eliminates mock Go binaries.

**Decision change**: After analysis, we are NOT mocking Keycloak or OpenBao. Instead:
- OpenBao dev mode (`openbao server -dev`) serves as both the secret store AND the OIDC identity provider
- OpenBao 2.5.0 (in nixos-unstable) supports `client_credentials` grant on its OIDC provider
- The `authn.OIDCVerifier` is IdP-agnostic — it just needs standard OIDC discovery, JWKS, and signed JWTs
- One service replaces two mock binaries

**Prerequisite**: Update nixpkgs flake input to pick up OpenBao 2.5.0 from nixos-unstable.

---

## Problem Space

**Axes of the problem:**
- Parallelism: Slices 1 (OpenBao OIDC config) and 2 (KV v2 provisioning) can run in a single provisioning script since they're both OpenBao CLI commands. Slice 3 (NixOS wiring) depends on slices 1+2.
- Distribution: All on the same host. Guest reaches OpenBao via VSOCK bridge.
- Cardinality: One service (OpenBao), one provisioning script, one NixOS integration pass.

**Has-a / Is-a:**
- OpenBao dev mode HAS-A root token (static, known at config time)
- OpenBao dev mode IS-A combined KV v2 store + OIDC provider
- Provisioning script IS-A oneshot systemd service (runs after OpenBao starts)
- Guest VSOCK bridge IS-A socat TCP-LISTEN → VSOCK-CONNECT

---

## Engineering Tradeoffs

| Option | Pros | Cons | Decision |
|--------|------|------|----------|
| Real OpenBao dev mode (OIDC + KV v2) | One service, no mock code, tests real APIs, production-path | Requires OpenBao 2.5.0 (flake update), provisioning script needed | **Selected** |
| Mock Go binaries (original plan) | Self-contained, no external deps | ~350 lines of new code, doesn't test real APIs, two extra binaries | Rejected |
| Keycloak + OpenBao | Tests real production stack | Heavyweight (Java+Postgres), 512MB+ RAM, slow boot | Rejected for MVP |
| Static JWT bypass | Simplest | Doesn't test auth pipeline end-to-end | Rejected |

| Decision | Choice | Rationale |
|----------|--------|-----------|
| IdP choice | OpenBao identity/oidc | Already needed for KV v2; 2.5.0 adds client_credentials; one service instead of two |
| Guest→OpenBao transport | VSOCK bridge to port 8200 | Guest needs to reach OIDC token endpoint. KV v2 is behind vault token auth so guest can't read secrets directly. |
| Dev mode token | Static root token | `openbao server -dev -dev-root-token-id=dev-token`. Provisioning script + credproxy use this token. |
| OIDC claims format | Template emitting `realm_access.roles` | Matches existing `authn.OIDCVerifier` Keycloak claim parsing. No Go code changes needed. |
| OpenBao version | 2.5.0 via flake update | Required for client_credentials grant. Available in nixos-unstable. |

---

## Slice 1: OpenBao OIDC Provider Configuration (`opa`)

### Provisioning script (part of NixOS oneshot service)

Configures OpenBao's identity/oidc engine to act as OIDC provider:

```bash
export BAO_ADDR="http://127.0.0.1:8200"
export BAO_TOKEN="dev-token"

# 1. Configure OIDC issuer
bao write identity/oidc/config issuer="http://127.0.0.1:8200"

# 2. Create signing key
bao write identity/oidc/key/credproxy-key \
  rotation_period=24h \
  allowed_client_ids="*"

# 3. Create identity entity for the VM agent
ENTITY_ID=$(bao write -format=json identity/entity \
  name="credproxy-agent" \
  metadata=role=agent | jq -r '.data.id')

# 4. Create OIDC assignment (maps entities to clients)
bao write identity/oidc/assignment/credproxy-assignment \
  entity_ids="$ENTITY_ID"

# 5. Create OIDC scope with realm_access template
# Template emits Keycloak-compatible claims so authn.OIDCVerifier works unchanged
bao write identity/oidc/scope/credproxy-scope \
  template='{"realm_access":{"roles":["credproxy-user"]},"groups":["openclaw-agents"]}'

# 6. Create confidential OIDC client
CLIENT_OUTPUT=$(bao write -format=json identity/oidc/client/credproxy \
  key=credproxy-key \
  assignments=credproxy-assignment \
  client_type=confidential \
  id_token_ttl=1h \
  access_token_ttl=1h)
CLIENT_ID=$(echo "$CLIENT_OUTPUT" | jq -r '.data.client_id')
CLIENT_SECRET=$(echo "$CLIENT_OUTPUT" | jq -r '.data.client_secret')

# 7. Create OIDC provider
bao write identity/oidc/provider/credproxy \
  scopes_supported=credproxy-scope \
  allowed_client_ids="$CLIENT_ID"

# 8. Write client credentials to file for guest provisioning
echo "CREDPROXY_CLIENT_ID=$CLIENT_ID" > /var/lib/credproxy/oidc-client.env
echo "CREDPROXY_CLIENT_SECRET=$CLIENT_SECRET" >> /var/lib/credproxy/oidc-client.env
chmod 600 /var/lib/credproxy/oidc-client.env
```

**OIDC endpoints served by OpenBao:**

| Endpoint | Path |
|----------|------|
| Discovery | `GET /v1/identity/oidc/provider/credproxy/.well-known/openid-configuration` |
| JWKS | `GET /v1/identity/oidc/provider/credproxy/.well-known/keys` |
| Token | `POST /v1/identity/oidc/provider/credproxy/token` |

**credproxy config change:**
```yaml
oidc:
  issuer_url: "http://127.0.0.1:8200/v1/identity/oidc/provider/credproxy"
  audience: "<client_id from provisioning>"
```

---

## Slice 2: OpenBao KV v2 Credential Provisioning (`8y4`)

### Provisioning script (same oneshot service as Slice 1)

```bash
# Enable KV v2 at "secret/" (dev mode may already have this)
bao secrets enable -path=secret -version=2 kv 2>/dev/null || true

# Write sample credentials
bao kv put secret/openclaw/credentials/httpbin \
  key="test-api-key-httpbin-12345" \
  header_name="Authorization" \
  header_prefix="Bearer "
```

**credproxy config:**
```yaml
vault:
  address: "http://127.0.0.1:8200"
  token: "dev-token"
```

---

## Slice 3: NixOS Integration + Sample Config (`71r`)

### Modified: `modules/credential-proxy/default.nix`

Add devMode options:

```nix
devMode = {
  enable = mkEnableOption "Dev mode with OpenBao dev server for auth + secrets";
  openbaoToken = mkOption { type = types.str; default = "dev-token"; };
  openbaoPort = mkOption { type = types.port; default = 8200; };
};
```

Add systemd services (gated on `cfg.devMode.enable`):

1. **credproxy-openbao-dev** — runs `openbao server -dev -dev-root-token-id=<token> -dev-listen-address=127.0.0.1:<port>`
2. **credproxy-openbao-provision** — oneshot After credproxy-openbao-dev, runs the provisioning script (OIDC setup + KV v2 secrets)
3. **credproxy** — gets `After=credproxy-openbao-provision.service` when devMode enabled

When devMode enabled, auto-set:
- `oidcIssuerURL = "http://127.0.0.1:${toString port}/v1/identity/oidc/provider/credproxy"`
- `vaultAddress = "http://127.0.0.1:${toString port}"`
- `vault.token = cfg.devMode.openbaoToken`

### Modified: `modules/credential-proxy/guest.nix`

Add VSOCK bridge for OpenBao auth:

```nix
systemd.services.credproxy-openbao-bridge = mkIf cfg.devMode.enable {
  description = "VSOCK Bridge to OpenBao for OIDC auth";
  serviceConfig.ExecStart = "${pkgs.socat}/bin/socat TCP-LISTEN:8200,fork,reuseaddr VSOCK-CONNECT:2:8200";
};
```

Update `credproxy-auth.sh` to support configurable token endpoint URL:
- `CREDPROXY_TOKEN_URL` defaults to `http://localhost:8200/v1/identity/oidc/provider/credproxy/token`
- Still uses `client_credentials` grant with `client_id` + `client_secret`
- Same output: JWT cached to `$CREDPROXY_TOKEN_FILE`

### Modified: `credential-proxy/flake.nix`

Update `vendorHash` if needed after flake input update.

### Modified: `flake.nix` (test-vm config)

```nix
CUSTOM.virtualisation.openclaw-vm.credentialProxy = {
  enable = true;
  devMode.enable = true;
  allowedDomains = [ "httpbin.org" ];
  credentials = [{
    placeholder = "agent-vault-00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000001";
    type = "api_key";
    vault_path = "secret/data/openclaw/credentials/httpbin";
    bound_domain = "httpbin.org";
    header_name = "Authorization";
    header_prefix = "Bearer ";
  }];
};
```

---

## Validation Checklist

### Phase 1 — OpenBao provisioning works standalone
- [ ] `openbao server -dev` starts and responds to health checks
- [ ] Provisioning script configures OIDC provider (discovery + JWKS endpoints respond)
- [ ] Provisioning script creates KV v2 secrets (GET returns correct envelope)
- [ ] `client_credentials` grant returns valid JWT
- [ ] JWT validates against `authn.OIDCVerifier` with OpenBao as issuer
- [ ] `vault.OpenBaoClient.FetchCredential()` returns correct CredentialValue
- [ ] Go tests pass: `go test -race ./...`
- [ ] ast-grep: zero violations

### Phase 2 — NixOS integration
- [ ] `nix flake check` passes with devMode enabled
- [ ] systemd service ordering correct (openbao-dev → provision → credproxy)
- [ ] Guest VSOCK bridge to port 8200 works
- [ ] credproxy-auth.sh obtains JWT via OpenBao OIDC

### Phase 3 — End-to-end
- [ ] VM boots with all services running
- [ ] Guest can authenticate and proxy a request to httpbin.org
- [ ] Response body has credential scrubbed

---

## BDD Acceptance Criteria

**Given** OpenBao dev mode running with OIDC provider "credproxy" configured
**When** `authn.NewOIDCVerifier(ctx, OIDCConfig{IssuerURL: "http://127.0.0.1:8200/v1/identity/oidc/provider/credproxy", Audience: "<client_id>"})` is called
**Then** verifier initializes successfully (OIDC discovery + JWKS fetch works)
**Should Not** fail due to missing OIDC endpoints or malformed JWKS

**Given** OpenBao OIDC provider running and verifier initialized
**When** JWT obtained via `client_credentials` grant and passed to `verifier.VerifyToken()`
**Then** returns AgentIdentity with Roles=["credproxy-user"]
**Should Not** return ErrTokenExpired, ErrInvalidIssuer, or ErrInvalidAudience

**Given** OpenBao dev mode with KV v2 secret at `secret/data/openclaw/credentials/httpbin`
**When** `vault.OpenBaoClient.FetchCredential(ctx, "secret/data/openclaw/credentials/httpbin")` is called
**Then** returns CredentialValue{Key: "test-api-key-httpbin-12345", HeaderName: "Authorization", HeaderPrefix: "Bearer "}
**Should Not** return ErrSecretNotFound

**Given** test-vm with devMode.enable=true
**When** `nix flake check` is run
**Then** module evaluation succeeds
**Should Not** produce type or unknown-option errors

---

## Files Affected

| File | Action | Description |
|------|--------|-------------|
| `modules/credential-proxy/default.nix` | modify | Add devMode options, OpenBao dev service, provisioning oneshot, auto-config wiring |
| `modules/credential-proxy/guest.nix` | modify | Add VSOCK bridge for OpenBao auth, update auth script env vars |
| `credential-proxy/scripts/credproxy-auth.sh` | modify | Support configurable token endpoint URL (OpenBao vs Keycloak) |
| `credential-proxy/flake.nix` | modify | Update vendorHash if needed |
| `flake.nix` | modify | Update nixpkgs input; enable credential proxy with devMode in test-vm |

No new Go source files. No mock binaries.

---

## Dependency Chain

```
Slice 1 (OIDC config) ──┐
                         ├──→ Slice 3 (NixOS wiring + sample creds) ──→ lvm (smoke test) ──→ coe (UAT)
Slice 2 (KV v2 creds) ──┘
```

Slices 1 and 2 are both part of the same provisioning script but are logically separable.

title: Replace stringly-typed APIs with strongly-typed enums across credential-proxy
description:
User requirement (Q31): All internal API boundaries must use strongly-typed enums, NOT string literals or untyped string constants.

Specific changes needed:

1. **ProxyStatus enum** (workflows/types.go):
   ```go
   type ProxyStatus int
   const (
       StatusSuccess ProxyStatus = iota
       StatusDenied
       StatusError
       StatusTimeout
   )
   func (s ProxyStatus) String() string { ... }
   ```

2. **DenialReason enum**:
   ```go
   type DenialReason int
   const (
       ReasonNone DenialReason = iota
       ReasonAuthenticationFailed
       ReasonAuthorizationDenied
       ReasonCredentialInjectionFailed
       ReasonTimeout
   )
   ```

3. **DecisionStatus enum** (already partially done by worker-temporal, verify):
   ```go
   type DecisionStatus int
   const (
       DecisionAllowed DecisionStatus = iota
       DecisionDenied
       DecisionError
   )
   ```

4. **Signal name constants**:
   ```go
   const SignalResponseComplete = "response_complete"
   ```

5. **WorkflowDecision** should carry typed DecisionStatus + DenialReason + HTTPStatusCode (int), not free-form string Reason.

6. **HTTP error mapping**: goproxy handler uses DenialReason → HTTP status code mapping (typed, not if/else on strings).

7. **Search attributes**: Go-side uses typed enums with .String() for Temporal serialization.

Files: credential-proxy/workflows/ (types, workflow, activities), credential-proxy/proxy/handlers.go, credential-proxy/audit/search_attributes.go
notes: Use `ast-grep` to find all stringly-typed values before making changes. ast-grep is available at /run/current-system/sw/bin/ast-grep. Write ast-grep patterns to identify: string literal function arguments, string-typed struct fields at API boundaries, bare string constants without named types.
close_reason: Replaced all stringly-typed status/decision/signal APIs with strongly-typed Go enums: ProxyStatus int, DecisionStatus int, DenialReason int with String()+HTTPStatusCode(), SignalName string type + SignalResponseComplete constant. All tests pass.

title: [SLICE-B] NixOS Integration + Guest Wiring (auth script, VSOCK bridge, flake config)
description:
## Production Code Path

**End user runs:** `credproxy-auth` (from guest VM) → obtains JWT via VSOCK bridge → OpenBao OIDC

**Guest lifecycle:** VSOCK bridge to OpenBao port 8200 starts → credproxy-auth reads client credentials → requests JWT via client_credentials grant → caches JWT to $CREDPROXY_TOKEN_FILE

## Worker Owns (Full Vertical Slice)

Plan backwards from production code path:
1. End: Guest agent runs `credproxy-auth`, obtains JWT, can make proxied requests
2. Back: credproxy-auth.sh uses CREDPROXY_TOKEN_URL to request JWT from OpenBao
3. Back: guest.nix provides VSOCK bridge to OpenBao + passes client credentials
4. Back: flake.nix enables credproxy with devMode in test-vm config

## Files You Own

- `modules/credential-proxy/guest.nix` — VSOCK bridge for OpenBao, token URL option, client creds via fw_cfg
- `credential-proxy/scripts/credproxy-auth.sh` — IdP-agnostic token URL
- `flake.nix` — test-vm config with devMode enabled, credential entries, nixpkgs lockfile update

## Implementation Details

### 1. Update credproxy-auth.sh (IdP-agnostic)

Replace hardcoded CREDPROXY_KEYCLOAK_URL with CREDPROXY_TOKEN_URL:
- Env var: `CREDPROXY_TOKEN_URL` (replaces CREDPROXY_KEYCLOAK_URL)
- Default: none (must be configured)
- Backward compat: if CREDPROXY_KEYCLOAK_URL is set and CREDPROXY_TOKEN_URL is not, use CREDPROXY_KEYCLOAK_URL
- Update all error messages from "Keycloak" to "token endpoint"
- Update comments/header to reflect IdP-agnostic design

### 2. Update guest.nix

**Add VSOCK bridge for OpenBao auth (devMode only):**
```nix
systemd.services.credproxy-openbao-bridge = mkIf cfg.devMode.enable {
  description = "VSOCK Bridge to OpenBao for OIDC auth";
  serviceConfig.ExecStart = "${pkgs.socat}/bin/socat TCP-LISTEN:8200,fork,reuseaddr VSOCK-CONNECT:2:8200";
};
```

**Rename keycloakURL option → tokenURL:**
- tokenURL option (replaces keycloakURL for IdP-agnostic design)
- When devMode enabled, auto-set to http://localhost:8200/v1/identity/oidc/provider/credproxy/token
- Update /etc/credproxy/client.env generation to use CREDPROXY_TOKEN_URL

**Pass OIDC client credentials via fw_cfg:**
- Host provisioning script writes client_id + client_secret to /var/lib/credproxy/oidc-client.env
- Add microvm.credentialFiles entry for oidc-client.env
- Guest reads at boot and populates CREDPROXY_CLIENT_ID + CREDPROXY_CLIENT_SECRET

**Add devMode option to guest config:**
```nix
devMode = {
  enable = mkEnableOption "Dev mode guest configuration (OpenBao VSOCK bridge, auto-config)";
};
```

### 3. Update flake.nix

**Enable credential proxy with devMode in test-vm:**
```nix
CUSTOM.virtualisation.openclaw-vm.credentialProxy = {
  enable = true;
  devMode.enable = true;
  allowedDomains = [ "httpbin.org" ];
  credentials = [{
    placeholder = "agent-vault-00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000001";
    type = "api_key";
    vault_path = "secret/data/openclaw/credentials/httpbin";
    bound_domain = "httpbin.org";
    header_name = "Authorization";
    header_prefix = "Bearer ";
  }];
};
```

**Update nixpkgs lockfile** if needed for OpenBao 2.5.0:
```bash
nix flake update nixpkgs
```

**Note:** credential-proxy/flake.nix vendorHash — only update if Go dependencies change. No new Go source files added, so vendorHash should be unchanged. Verify with `nix build .#credential-proxy`.

## Key Design Decisions

- credproxy-auth.sh remains backward compatible (CREDPROXY_KEYCLOAK_URL fallback)
- Guest devMode option mirrors host devMode option
- VSOCK bridge for OpenBao uses port 8200 (standard OpenBao/Vault port)
- Client credentials delivered to guest same way as placeholder env vars (fw_cfg)

## Depends On

- Slice-A (1fq): devMode options must exist in default.nix; provisioning must write client credentials file

## Validation Checklist

Before marking complete:
- [ ] credproxy-auth.sh uses CREDPROXY_TOKEN_URL env var
- [ ] credproxy-auth.sh backward compatible with CREDPROXY_KEYCLOAK_URL
- [ ] guest.nix VSOCK bridge to OpenBao port 8200 (devMode only)
- [ ] guest.nix auto-configures token URL when devMode enabled
- [ ] guest.nix passes client credentials to guest via fw_cfg
- [ ] flake.nix test-vm config has devMode enabled with httpbin credential
- [ ] nix flake check passes
- [ ] No TODO placeholders
- [ ] No hardcoded Keycloak references in credproxy-auth.sh
design:
{
    "productionCodePath": "credproxy-auth → VSOCK bridge → OpenBao OIDC → JWT",
    "validation_checklist": [
      "nix flake check passes",
      "credproxy-auth.sh uses CREDPROXY_TOKEN_URL",
      "credproxy-auth.sh backward compatible with CREDPROXY_KEYCLOAK_URL",
      "guest.nix VSOCK bridge for OpenBao (devMode only)",
      "flake.nix test-vm config with devMode enabled",
      "No TODO placeholders",
      "No hardcoded Keycloak references in auth script"
    ],
    "acceptance_criteria": [
      {
        "given": "guest VM with devMode enabled",
        "when": "credproxy-auth is run",
        "then": "obtains JWT via OpenBao OIDC through VSOCK bridge",
        "should_not": "fail due to hardcoded Keycloak URL"
      },
      {
        "given": "test-vm with devMode.enable=true",
        "when": "nix flake check is run",
        "then": "module evaluation succeeds",
        "should_not": "produce type or unknown-option errors"
      }
    ],
    "ratified_plan": "6ce",
    "covers_tasks": ["71r"],
    "depends_on_slice": "1fq"
  }

title: CREDENTIAL-PROXY: Implement credential proxy security layer for openclaw-vm
description:
## Goal
Implement a credential proxy security layer for the openclaw microVM where:
- Agent inside VM has zero external network access
- All auth-required requests delegated to trusted proxy service on host via VSOCK
- Credentials stored in OpenBao with domain binding enforcement
- Proxy injects auth headers — agent never sees API keys
- Full audit trail of all credential access

## Architecture
Host (NixOS)
├── OpenBao (secrets store)
│   └── Credentials with domain binding metadata
├── Credential Proxy Service (NEW)
│   ├── Listens on VSOCK
│   ├── Validates ACP protocol requests
│   ├── Fetches credential from OpenBao + verifies domain binding
│   ├── Injects auth headers into outbound HTTPS request
│   ├── Templates response
│   └── Audit logs everything
└── openclaw-vm (microVM)
    └── Agent (zero network — all via VSOCK → proxy)

## Phases
1. Protocol & Types (ACP subset)
2. Credential Proxy Service (Rust)
3. NixOS Integration
4. Guest-Side Client
5. VM Network Lockdown
notes: Reopened: Feature must remain open until user performs UAT.

title: Fix: guest VSOCK bridge hardcodes port 8200 instead of reading devMode.openbaoPort
description:
Both post-fix reviewers flagged this pre-existing issue (not introduced by ef84e6d):

guest.nix:261 runs:
  socat TCP-LISTEN:8200,fork,reuseaddr VSOCK-CONNECT:2:8200

The port 8200 is hardcoded rather than reading from a guest-side NixOS option. If an operator sets host devMode.openbaoPort != 8200, the host listens on the new port but the guest still connects to 8200 — silent misconfiguration.

Fix: add a guest devMode.openbaoPort option (default 8200) and use it in credproxy-openbao-bridge ExecStart. The host module should propagate the value via microvm.vms.openclaw-vm.config when devMode is enabled.

title: Add RequestRegistry TTL-based eviction and connTokens cleanup
description:
Two memory leak paths found by reviewers:

1. RequestRegistry: if goproxy panics between workflow start and response, the entry leaks (defer may not run). Add a background goroutine that evicts entries older than 2x signal timeout.

2. connTokens (sync.Map storing JWT per CONNECT): if client disconnects after CONNECT but before sending a request, the entry leaks. handleResponse is the only cleanup path. Add TTL-based eviction (time.AfterFunc on store, cancel on delete).

title: REVIEW W2-1: Correctness review of wave 2 slices
description:
## Review Axis: Correctness (spirit and technicality)

Review ALL wave 2 slices against the PROPOSAL and URD:

### Slice 0 (h80): goleak + race detector
- Are goleak ignores justified? Do they mask real leaks?
- Is -race flag properly wired in Nix checkPhase?

### Slice 1 (glo): Decision pipeline hardening
- Does select+timeout on DecisionCh actually prevent deadlocks in all code paths?
- Does moving JWT validation to goproxy correctly handle all error cases (expired, invalid issuer, JWKS unavailable)?
- Are structured error types (DenialReason → HTTP status) mapped correctly? (407 for authn, 403 for authz, 502 for vault, 504 for timeout)
- Does the roundtrip integration test cover the full goproxy→Temporal→Registry→vault→scrub cycle?
- Are there any workflow exit paths that DON'T send on DecisionCh?

### Slice 2 (5rc): TTL eviction
- Does the sweeper actually prevent memory leaks under sustained load?
- Are there race conditions between sweeper eviction and normal request completion?
- Does connToken timer cancellation prevent double-delete races?

### Slice 3 (m1b): nftables Tailscale exemption
- Does `oifname "tailscale0" accept` correctly exempt Tailscale SSH?
- Is the rule conditional on cfg.tailscale.enable?
- Does the rule placement (before drop) work correctly?

### Slice 4 (3mu): TLA+ model
- Does the model faithfully represent the actual code's concurrency protocol?
- Are the invariants (NoDeadlock, EveryRequestGetsResponse, etc.) correctly specified?
- Does TLC pass with zero violations?

### Cross-slice concerns
- Are there file conflicts or inconsistencies between slices?
- Do all slices follow the type safety rules (no stringly-typed APIs)?
- Does `ast-grep scan --config sgconfig.yml .` pass with zero violations?
